6.16.26

GEOPOLITICS & ENERGY SHOCK
- US-Iran Conflict & Breakthrough: Markets are digesting a massive breakthrough after President Trump announced late Sunday on Truth Social that a "complete" interim deal with Iran has been finalized. The agreement halts hostilities immediately on all fronts (including Lebanon) and outlines a 60-day negotiation window focused on Tehran's nuclear program. A formal signing ceremony is expected this Friday in Switzerland, hosted by Pakistan.
- Strait of Hormuz Reopening: Trump announced he has authorized the toll-free reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, with the US naval blockade set to end. However, shipowners, insurers, and maritime analysts remain highly cautious. Normalizing traffic and clearing mines from the waterway among the ~600 stranded vessels is expected to take months.
- Nuclear & Sanctions Friction: The deal defers the fate of Iran's nuclear program to technical talks. Trump told the NY Times he will instantly restart military strikes if Iran fails to reach a final nuclear accord. Iranian media claims $24B in frozen assets will be released over the 60 days (50% upfront) alongside oil sanctions waivers, though US officials insist relief is strictly performance-dependent. Regional risk remains high as Israeli PM Netanyahu told Trump that Israel is not bound by the Lebanon clause and will continue its southern campaign against Hezbollah.
- Crude Oil Collapse: WTI and Brent crude plummeted nearly 5% to three-month lows, with Brent dropping below $82/bbl and testing its 200-DMA for the first time since February. Middle Eastern physical crude benchmarks collapsed, and the forward curve for Dubai and Murban flipped into contango (prompt discount), signaling a major easing of immediate supply anxiety. Both Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley aggressively cut their structural oil price outlooks.
- Regional Energy Plays: Qatar plans a rapid expansion of LNG exports once safe passage is normalized, targeting 50% capacity within one month and 80% within two months. Concurrently, Canadian wildfires in Alberta are burning within 11-19 km of critical oil sands facilities, threatening 500,000 bpd of crude production. 
- WTI OpEx: The front-month futures roll occurs today at 2:30 p.m. ET as August crude (CLQ6) becomes the front-month contract over July (CLN6).

MACROECONOMICS, RATES & FEDERAL RESERVE
- The Warsh FOMC Debut: Newly sworn-in Fed Chair Kevin Warsh faces his maiden policy meeting on Wednesday. While the central bank is universally expected to hold rates flat at 3.50%-3.75% (>98% probability), the updated Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) and dot plot are expected to show a hawkish revision. 
- Rate Hike Adjustments: Following May's blockbuster payroll print (unemployment steady at 4.3%), macro models have aggressively shifted. Swap markets are currently pricing a 5% chance of a hike this week, a 50% chance by October, and an 80% chance by year-end. Policymakers enter a media blackout as investors look to see if Warsh will roll back forward guidance and dismantle the traditional dot plot architecture.
- Inflation Shock Day: US May CPI prints today at 8:30 a.m. ET, with the annual headline figure projected to jump to a 3-year high of 4.2% on war-driven energy, jet fuel, and metal spikes. JPMorgan's Market Intelligence desk warns that a core MoM print above 0.35% could trigger an immediate 3% drop in the S&P 500, while a standard print implies a 1% index move.
- Volatility Curve Inversion: The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) plunged 5.2% to 16.76 in a sharp reversal from recent highs above 23.38. However, near-term anxiety remains intense, with the 3-month VIX curve shifting into backwardation. 
- International Central Banking: 
  - BOJ: Normalizing policy in a 7-1 vote, the Bank of Japan raised its benchmark interest rate to a 31-year high of 1.0% to combat structural energy inflation, though it paused its bond taper program to continue buying 2 trillion yen in JGBs monthly. Dovish board member Toichiro Asada dissented.
  - ECB: President Lagarde defended recent rate hikes to 2.25%, warning that indirect energy shocks are embedding across European travel services and utilities. The Bundesbank slashed German growth forecasts, warning of prolonged stagnation.
  - Bank of Canada: Expected to hold its key policy rate steady at 2.25% on Wednesday. The Canadian economy has entered a technical recession (two straight quarters of contraction), though institutions like CIBC expect the bank to downplay the slide due to a strong Q2 rebound.

EQUITIES, SPACE MANIA & THE AI CAPITAL RECALIBRATION
- SpaceX Multi-Trillion Run: Following its blockbuster public debut, SpaceX rocketed 20% in a single session, adding $433 billion in market value (the second-largest one-day market cap gain in US corporate history). SpaceX sits at a $2.538 trillion valuation—just 4% behind Amazon. Elon Musk announced that SpaceX could hit $1 trillion in annual revenue by 2030, hitting a post-IPO valuation over $2 trillion, despite currently posting a $4.94 billion net loss on $18.67 billion in 2025 revenue.
- Anysphere Acquisition: Broadening its enterprise AI stack, SpaceX acquired Anysphere, the startup behind the popular AI coding assistant Cursor, for $60 billion. Retail demand for SpaceX stock has been historic, matching the entire retail purchasing volume of the US stock market last week. Listed options launch today, featuring call strikes stretching to $380 for this Thursday's expiration.
- Frontier AI Listings: The era of stock scarcity has broken. OpenAI has confidentially filed for its US IPO, while rival Anthropic prepares a concurrent listing, both eyeing ~$1 trillion public valuations. Databricks is in discussions for a fresh funding round at a $ 165B-$ 175B valuation, targeting an IPO next year. China's top AI startup, DeepSeek, finalized a massive $7.4 billion funding round backed by Tencent and CATL, valuing the entity at over $50 billion.
- The Token Minimization Era: In his closing analysis, Mike O'Rorke highlights a fundamental structural shift in corporate artificial intelligence adoption. Major hyperscalers (Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) are executing "Tokenminimizing" strategies, actively rationing expensive AI consumption and switching to less expensive, "good enough" open models. This emergence of an LLM pricing war—coupled with Oracle guiding its fiscal capex up 27%—signals that enterprise infrastructure monetization will take much longer to materialize than ebullient consensus models assume.
- Anthropic National Security Ban: Adding a major bottleneck to the tech sector, the US government issued an emergency export control order forcing Anthropic to completely block all foreign nationals from accessing its advanced "Mythos" and "Fable" AI models. The restriction was triggered by Amazon researchers proving that safety guardrails could be bypassed to uncover sovereign software vulnerabilities. 
- Corporate Equity Supply: Goldman Sachs warns that a historic deluge of ~$1.5 trillion in new equity issuance via mega-IPOs and corporate secondary sales over the next two years will fundamentally reverse the 20-year tailwind of net-negative US stock supply. 
- Technical Index Flows: The CRSP Index Reconstitution begins today through June 22, directly impacting over $1 trillion in active and passive US domestic equities. Marvell (MRVL) and Flex (FLEX) are surging premarket on news they will join the S&P 500 later this month.

MARKET MOVERS & CORPORATE ACTIONS
- Energy Retrenchment vs. Travel Surge: Battered travel, airline, and hospitality names are leading premarket gainers on the back of the peace deal (United +4.3%, Delta +3.9%, American +3.5%, Norwegian Cruise +4.0%). Conversely, megacap oil and exploration names are sliding heavily (Occidental -3.3%, Exxon -2.5%, Chevron -2.4%). 
- Mining Inflows: Easing Middle East tensions have allowed spot gold to stabilize around $4,330/bbl, allowing precious metal miners to catch a strong bid (Pan American Silver +4.8%, Newmont +4.7%, Barrick +4.3%).
- M&A & Antitrust Clearances: 
  - FOXA/ROKU: Fox Corp officially agreed to acquire Roku in a cash-and-stock transaction valued at $160/share ($22 billion enterprise value). The deal will be backed by $12 billion in committed bridge financing.
  - PARA/WBD: The US Department of Justice completed its 8-month antitrust review and fully cleared Paramount's massive $110 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery.
  - Woodside Energy (WDS): Denied it is holding active talks with ExxonMobil, though internal leaks show Exxon is evaluating the Australian LNG giant as an acquisition target.
- Super Micro Offering: Super Micro Computer (SMCI) announced a giant $7 billion capital raise—comprising a $5 billion underwritten package and a $2 billion open-market ATM program—to secure liquid-cooling components and hardware for its enterprise AI server backlogs.
- GitLab (GTLB): Beating Q1 expectations with revenue of $264.2 million, the software platform raised its full-year guidance on agentic AI tailwinds but simultaneously announced a 14% workforce reduction and exit from 22 countries.

DOMESTIC & GLOBAL POLITICS
- Multi-State Primaries Today: High-stakes primary voting commences today in Maine, South Carolina, Nevada, and North Dakota. In Maine, five-term Republican Senator Susan Collins faces intense reelection pressure over macro inflation, while her challenger Graham Platner battles a public texting scandal. In South Carolina, Rep. Nancy Mace faces a critical base test after demanding the release of Jeffrey Epstein documents.
- UK Social Media Ban: Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced an aggressive "full ban" prohibiting children under the age of 16 from using social media platforms, targeting stranger interaction and livestreaming mechanisms by spring 2027.
- USMCA Integration: Ontario Premier Doug Ford is heading to Washington to launch a "Fortress North America" integration pitch with US officials ahead of the July 1 USMCA revision deadline.
- Swiss Population Referendum: Switzerland holds a national referendum this Sunday on a hard population cap of 10 million people, a right-wing push that corporations warn could trigger severe labor and macroeconomic restrictions.

CALENDAR
- Mon (15th): June NY Empire State Manufacturing, US May Industrial Production, Capacity Utilization, NAHB Housing Market Index. G7 Leaders' Summit begins in Evian, France. Block Trade: 3M shares of CAVA.
- Tue (16th): China Retail Sales, Industrial Production, Fixed-Asset Investment, Property Prices. Germany ZEW Survey. US Housing Starts & Building Permits, Import Price Index. CRSP Reconstitution begins. WTI Crude OpEx (August CLQ6 becomes front-month). API Petroleum Data. Earnings: PANW, SJM, CASY.
- Wed (17th): Eurozone/UK May CPI, ECB Wage Tracker. Japan Core Machinery Orders & Trade Balance. IEA Monthly Oil Market Report. US May Retail Sales, Business Inventories, Pending Home Sales, MBA Mortgage Applications. FOMC Interest Rate Decision & Dots (2:00 p.m. ET) followed by Chair Warsh's maiden press conference (2:30 p.m. ET). Brazil Central Bank Interest Rate Decision. Earnings: ORCL, JBL.
- Thu (18th): Global Quarterly Witching Expiration (1 day early due to Juneteenth holiday). Bank of England (BOE), Swiss National Bank (SNB), Norges Bank, Czech, and Indonesia Central Bank Interest Rate Decisions. UK Jobless Claims & Unemployment. UK Makerfield By-Election. US Initial Jobless Claims, Leading Index, Cross-Border TIC Flows.
- Fri (19th): US Markets Closed for Juneteenth Holiday (Equities, Credit, and DTCC Settlement Closed). Official US-Iran Peace Agreement Signing Ceremony in Geneva, Switzerland. Japan May CPI, BOJ April Meeting Minutes. UK Retail Sales, GfK Consumer Confidence. Canada Retail Sales.

Wind Financial Morning Post: June 16, 2026

Market Brief

Trump: Details of the U.S.-Iran agreement will be disclosed after it is formally signed on June 19.

Israel says the U.S.-Iran agreement is “not binding” on Israel, and that Iran will not possess nuclear weapons.
Iran will charge fees for shipping services related to the Strait of Hormuz.

Global stock markets rallied on Monday: the Dow hit another record high, and China’s ChiNext Index rose more than 5%.

International oil prices fell more than 4% after the U.S. and Iran reached an agreement, while gold returned to the $4,300 level.

U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve falls to a nearly 43-year low, with plans to replenish 200 million barrels over the next year.

SpaceX’s valuation surpasses $2.5 trillion, with total IPO fundraising rising to $85.7 billion.

Nvidia issues investment-grade bonds for the first time in five years, planning to raise $25 billion.

Top Stories

The memorandum of understanding on a ceasefire reached between the United States and Iran has already been signed electronically, with a formal signing ceremony scheduled for June 19 in Geneva. Some provisions of the memorandum will begin taking effect on the 15th, while others, including the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, will take effect after the formal signing on the 19th. U.S. President Donald Trump said the contents of the U.S.-Iran agreement will be disclosed after the formal signing. Iran will be allowed to conduct limited, low-level uranium enrichment. Iran’s Foreign Ministry said asset unfreezing and war compensation are priorities in the memorandum, that Lebanon is an inseparable part of the Iran-U.S. ceasefire agreement, and that nuclear talks and sanctions relief will begin within 60 days after the memorandum is signed. In addition, Iran will charge fees for shipping services related to the Strait of Hormuz.

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said that, after intensive discussions, an overwhelming majority of members of Iran’s Islamic Consultative Assembly agreed to the text of the Iran-U.S. memorandum of understanding, as a practical test of the United States’ genuine determination to respect the rights of the Iranian people. Pezeshkian noted that the contents agreed in the memorandum are an important step toward ending the war and opening negotiations, but that a final agreement has not yet been reached. Iran is prepared for all possible options.

In response to the memorandum of understanding between the United States and Iran, Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir said the agreement reached by the U.S. and Iran is “not binding” on Israel. 

Israel would “never accept” anything short of the complete dismantling of Hezbollah in Lebanon, and Israel will not withdraw from occupied territories. Sources revealed that, during a phone call between Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and U.S. President Donald Trump, Netanyahu made clear that Israel does not consider itself bound by the Lebanon-related provisions in the U.S.-Iran agreement. Netanyahu also said that, with or without this agreement, Iran will never be allowed to possess nuclear weapons.

Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, the underwriters for SpaceX’s IPO, exercised a “greenshoe” over-allotment option for 83.3 million shares, pushing SpaceX’s total fundraising to $85.7 billion and continuing to set new global IPO records. On Monday, SpaceX shares closed up 19.60%, sending its market capitalization above $2.5 trillion. SpaceX CEO Elon Musk said the company could reach $1 trillion in revenue by 2030.

China Macro & Policy

China’s State Council held its 20th special study session on the theme of “strengthening the implementation of the major function-oriented zoning strategy and promoting coordinated regional development.” Premier Li Qiang, while presiding over the session, stressed the need to further improve the major function-oriented zoning system, promote a new pattern of territorial spatial development and protection featuring clearly defined main functions, complementary strengths, and high-quality development, and provide strong support for advancing coordinated regional development and optimizing the layout of major productive forces.

The State Council’s work safety assessment and inspection teams recently visited eight provincial-level regions, including Hebei, Shanxi, Heilongjiang, Anhui, Hunan, Guangxi, Chongqing, and Xinjiang, to carry out strict, practical, and detailed open and covert inspections for the second and third quarters. The teams will take a serious and rigorous approach, confront problems head-on, crack down hard on all types of illegal and unlawful activities, and resolutely prevent and curb major and serious accidents.

The Hong Kong SAR Government has launched a public consultation on Hong Kong’s first five-year plan for economic and social development for 2026-2030, covering areas such as the economy, industries, spatial planning, infrastructure, green transition, as well as people’s livelihoods and well-being, including healthcare, education, housing, welfare, and elderly care.

China Equities Markets

China’s A-share market surged, with the Shanghai Composite Index closing up 1.61% at 4,096.47 points, the Shenzhen Component Index rising 3.79%, the ChiNext Index jumping 5.3%, the STAR 50 Index gaining 5.12%, and the Wind All A Index up 2.95%. Market turnover exceeded RMB 3 trillion. On the market, the AI industrial chain rallied across the board, with copper-clad laminates, printed circuit boards, optical modules, semiconductor silicon wafers, and HBM-related concepts leading the gains, while coal, banking, and oil and gas stocks were among the biggest decliners.

Hong Kong stocks closed higher after a volatile session. The Hang Seng Index rose 0.5% to 24,842.67 points, the Hang Seng Tech Index gained 1.28%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index edged up 0.02%. AI application stocks led the gains, with Zhipu surging more than 32%. Semiconductors, hardware equipment, and non-ferrous metals were among the top-performing sectors, while coal stocks led the losses. Southbound funds recorded net buying of HKD 2.1 billion, with Zhipu seeing additional purchases of nearly HKD 2.3 billion.

Since June, institutional research interest in A-shares has heated up significantly, with funds actively positioning in high-quality market targets. Judging from research trends, high-growth sectors such as semiconductors and optoelectronics have attracted concentrated institutional visits. Since June, a total of 338 listed companies have received institutional research visits, among which 15 companies, including Jereh Group, Dajin Heavy Industry, Shanghai Hanxun, TCL Technology, and Rongda Photosensitive, were visited by 50 or more institutions. In terms of research topics, listed companies’ global expansion strategies have drawn strong institutional attention.

According to Wind data, as of June 15, nearly 600 companies had carried out share buybacks this year, with the total repurchase amount approaching RMB 58 billion. Among them, Midea Group had repurchased RMB 4.939 billion, the highest amount; BOE Technology Group A and SF Holding had repurchased RMB 4.729 billion and RMB 4.237 billion, respectively.

Two leading chip companies passed their IPO reviews on the same day. Information on the stock exchange websites showed that the IPOs of Enflame Technology and CanSemi Technology were both approved by the listing committee. Enflame Technology is one of China’s “four domestic GPU unicorns” and plans to list on the STAR Market, raising RMB 6 billion. CanSemi Technology plans to raise RMB 7.5 billion and is expected to become the first wafer manufacturer listed on the ChiNext Market.

Taiwan’s weighted index closed up 2.78% at 45,396.99 points. MediaTek rose nearly 7%, while TSMC and Hon Hai gained nearly 3%.

China Industry News

Five government departments have launched a three-year campaign to tackle energy-saving and carbon-reduction upgrades in key industries. Starting in 2026, comprehensive upgrades will be carried out across nine major energy-intensive industries: steel, electrolytic aluminum, cement, flat glass, oil refining, ethylene, synthetic ammonia, methanol, and coal-fired power. Capacity that still fails to meet energy-efficiency standards by the end of 2028 will be eliminated and shut down in accordance with laws and regulations.

China has, for the first time, successfully achieved independent mass production of silicon-28 isotopes with an abundance of more than 99.99%. Key product indicators have reached internationally advanced levels, which will support China’s independent development of core materials for silicon-based quantum computing, as well as frontier technology fields such as advanced-process semiconductors and high-end navigation.

MLCCs may be heading into the “longest shortage cycle in history.” According to reports, the MLCC shortage has begun to spread, with shortages expanding from high-end products to mid- and low-end categories. Suppliers planning capacity expansions are being constrained by equipment bottlenecks, and the industry expects the shortage may last until 2027 or even 2028.

According to the State Post Bureau, China’s express delivery development index stood at 472.9 in May, up 3.1% year on year. By sub-index, express delivery volume in May is estimated to have increased by around 6% year on year, while express delivery revenue is estimated to have grown by around 8.5%.

Fintech

Zhou Yanli, chairman of the special committee of the Zhongguancun Fintech Industry Development Alliance and former vice chairman of the China Insurance Regulatory Commission, recently said that regulators should, based on the trend of digital and intelligent transformation, improve fintech regulatory rules, clarify the boundaries of innovation and compliance requirements, avoid a “one-size-fits-all” regulatory approach, and leave reasonable room for innovative development.

The Philippine central bank has issued new regulatory guidelines for virtual assets, requiring all licensed virtual asset service providers to establish strict due diligence and asset review mechanisms before listing or trading any crypto asset. The new rules also explicitly prohibit “anonymity-enhanced virtual assets.”

Corporate News & Updates

Nvidia is returning to the investment-grade bond market after about five years. The company plans to raise $25 billion through the issuance of senior bonds. The offering attracted about $85 billion in orders, and the yield on the longest-dated tranche narrowed by 0.25 percentage points from initial price guidance, with the final spread set at 0.65 percentage points above U.S. Treasuries.

In response to a series of food safety issues recently identified by regulators and exposed by the media at Sam’s Club offline stores and online shops, China’s State Administration for Market Regulation summoned the head of Sam’s Club headquarters for a regulatory accountability meeting in accordance with the law. The regulator required the company to strictly prevent food safety risks across the entire chain and effectively safeguard public food safety.

Meta recently released an internal memo disclosing that the company expects spending on “internal AI use” alone to reach several billion dollars in 2026, and plans to formally implement a token management mechanism centered on budgets and quotas in 2027.

Fox Corporation will acquire streaming video platform Roku for about $22 billion. After the acquisition is completed, Fox will gain direct access to Roku’s massive existing user base of more than 100 million streaming households, helping it deliver targeted advertising more precisely and reduce its reliance on traditional TV distribution channels.

A Starbucks promotion in South Korea seriously backfired, leading to a significant decline in sales, and the CEO of Starbucks Korea was dismissed as a result. Starbucks Korea announced that it will close all stores nationwide at 3 p.m. local time on June 22 to conduct training for all employees.

Global Macro & Policy

The EU and Ukraine have formally launched the first negotiating chapter for Ukraine’s accession to the bloc. The EU and Ukraine officially began talks on the “fundamentals” cluster in Ukraine’s EU accession negotiations. According to the announcement, the “fundamentals” cluster covers the EU’s core values and basic principles, including EU laws and regulations in areas such as the rule of law and fundamental rights, the functioning of democratic institutions, public administration reform, and economic criteria.

French President Emmanuel Macron, responding to U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariff threat, said France will not bow to U.S. pressure and cancel its digital tax targeting American tech giants. Trump previously said the U.S. would impose a 100% tariff on French wine unless France scraps its digital tax on U.S. tech giants.

U.S. manufacturing output was flat month on month in May, ending four consecutive months of growth and falling short of market expectations. U.S. industrial production rose only 0.1% month on month in May, compared with expectations for a 0.3% increase, while the previous reading was revised to a 0.9% gain.

The Bank of Japan’s interest-rate decision is due today, with markets widely expecting the central bank to raise rates by 25 basis points to 1%. This would mark Japan’s first return to the 1% interest-rate era since 1995. At the same time, markets will closely watch for clues on when the BOJ may act again and monitor the yen’s performance.

European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde said high energy prices are spreading to other areas of the economy, with risks of “second-round effects,” especially wage increases, emerging. ECB Governing Council member Joachim Nagel said that even if the Strait of Hormuz resumes navigation soon, it will take months for oil supply to return to normal, and all options will remain open at the July meeting.

Turkey and Saudi Arabia plan to build a railway over the next three to four years connecting the two countries with Jordan and Syria, and extending directly to Europe. The railway is expected to help ease transportation difficulties caused by shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. The UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Oman, Yemen, and other countries may also join the project in the future.

The South Korean government has officially launched a “Super Innovation Economy Project,” planning to invest KRW 500 billion in state funds to tackle next-generation power semiconductor technologies. Including matching private-sector funding, the total size of the R&D project is expected to expand to KRW 750 billion.

British Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced that people under the age of 16 will be banned from using certain social media platforms. Starmer said he hopes to pass the legislation before the end of the year, with the ban expected to take effect next spring.

Global Equity Markets

The three major U.S. stock indexes all closed higher. The Dow rose 0.92% to 51,671.03, setting another record high; the S&P 500 gained 1.65% to 7,554.29; and the Nasdaq climbed 3.07% to 26,683.94. Boeing rose more than 4% and Nvidia gained over 3%, leading the Dow. The Wind U.S. Magnificent Seven Index rose 2.71%, with Facebook up more than 4% and Amazon gaining over 3%. SpaceX surged 19.6%, lifting its total market capitalization to $2.52 trillion. The Nasdaq Golden Dragon China Index rose 0.39%, with Silvercorp Metals up more than 8% and Autohome gaining over 6%.

The three major European stock indexes ended mixed. Germany’s DAX rose 1.05% to 24,894.01; France’s CAC 40 gained 0.40% to 8,384.01; and the UK’s FTSE 100 fell 0.39% to 10,430.62. A temporary agreement between the U.S. and Iran to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz boosted market risk appetite. However, the energy sector was dragged down by a sharp drop in oil prices, with oil stocks such as Shell and BP declining, putting pressure on the FTSE 100.

Major Asia-Pacific stock indexes closed higher. South Korea’s KOSPI rose 5.2% to 8,545.98, triggering circuit breakers for the sixth consecutive trading day. Japan’s Nikkei 225 gained 4.99% to 69,317.5, closing above 69,000 for the first time. AI-related stocks rallied across the board, with Kioxia rising nearly 12%, SoftBank Group gaining more than 10%, Samsung Electronics up 4.5%, and SK Hynix climbing over 6%. India’s SENSEX 30 rose 0.97% to 76,264.33.

Data from the Bank of Korea showed that foreign securities investment funds recorded a net outflow of $26.15 billion in May, marking the fourth consecutive month of net outflows. Equity funds saw a net outflow of $31.83 billion, a record high for a single month. Since the beginning of this year, total foreign outflows from the stock market have reached $77.83 billion.

Bonds

China’s interbank bond market was largely range-bound, with yields on major interest-rate bonds mixed. Bonds with maturities of five years and above remained resilient, while those with maturities of one to three years were relatively weak. Main government bond futures contracts all rose, with the 30-year contract up 0.25%. Liquidity conditions in the interbank market tightened further. The central bank conducted RMB 425 billion in reverse repo operations, resulting in a net injection of RMB 206.5 billion.

Data from the PBOC Shanghai Head Office showed that as of the end of May 2026, overseas institutions held RMB 3.21 trillion of bonds in China’s interbank market, accounting for about 1.8% of the total custody volume in the interbank bond market.

U.S. Treasury yields were mixed. The 2-year Treasury yield fell 1.25 basis points to 4.066%, the 3-year yield fell 2.24 basis points to 4.114%, the 5-year yield fell 1.76 basis points to 4.190%, the 10-year yield fell 1.19 basis points to 4.473%, while the 30-year yield rose 0.90 basis point to 4.979%.

Commodities

The Shanghai Gold Exchange issued a notice stating that, from the close-of-day clearing on June 17, the margin ratio for contracts including Au(T+D) will be adjusted from 15% to 16%, and the daily price limit will be adjusted from 14% to 15% starting from the next trading day. For the Ag(T+D) contract, the margin ratio will be adjusted from 21% to 22%, and the daily price limit will be adjusted from 20% to 21% starting from the next trading day.

International precious metals futures broadly closed higher. COMEX gold futures rose 2.18% to $4,331 per ounce, while COMEX silver futures gained 3.07% to $70.06 per ounce. The U.S.-Iran peace agreement eased geopolitical risks and inflation concerns, while central bank gold purchases and loose liquidity also provided support.

International oil prices fell across the board. The main WTI crude contract dropped 4.38% to $81.16 per barrel, while the main Brent crude contract fell 4.55% to $83.36 per barrel. The U.S.-Iran peace agreement significantly eased the geopolitical risk premium. Combined with a weak global demand outlook and market concerns over oversupply, multiple bearish factors drove a pullback in oil prices.

Most London base metals rose. LME tin futures gained 2.97% to $55,350.0 per tonne, LME copper rose 0.72% to $13,796.5 per tonne, LME lead edged up 0.13% to $1,968.5 per tonne, and LME zinc inched up 0.01% to $3,584.5 per tonne. LME nickel fell 0.08% to $17,815.0 per tonne, while LME aluminum dropped 4.30% to $3,383.0 per tonne.

The Trump administration is accelerating the implementation of a 172-million-barrel crude oil release plan to hedge against the oil price surge caused by the U.S.-Iran war, pushing U.S. emergency crude supplies to their lowest level since 1983. Data from the U.S. Department of Energy showed that the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), established during the Arab oil embargo of the 1970s, has fallen to about 340 million barrels, approaching a record low. The Department of Energy plans to replenish about 200 million barrels over the next year, around 20% more than the current release volume.

The London Bullion Market Association (LBMA) is considering moving its morning gold auction earlier to better accommodate Asian traders. Currently, the LBMA Gold Price is set twice daily through an electronic platform, at 10:30 and 15:00 London time, and is denominated in U.S. dollars. The price serves as a benchmark used by global miners, refiners, investors, and central banks.

Singapore will launch an over-the-counter (OTC) gold clearing system this year and will also provide vault custody services for central banks, aiming to build itself into a global precious metals trading hub. Several major international banks, including JPMorgan Chase, Deutsche Bank, and DBS Bank, will participate in the system.

Citi raised its three-month gold price forecast to $4,500 per ounce and its silver price forecast to $70 per ounce. Citi lowered its oil price expectations to its previous bearish-case levels, with its latest quarterly oil price forecasts for the third and fourth quarters of 2026 at $75 and $70 per barrel, respectively.

FX & Currency Markets

On Monday, the onshore yuan closed at 6.7572 against the U.S. dollar at 16:30, up 44 basis points from the previous trading day and reaching its highest level since early February 2023. It closed at 6.7568 in night trading. The yuan’s central parity rate against the dollar was set at 6.8088, up 21 basis points from the previous trading day.

In late New York trading, the U.S. Dollar Index fell 0.13% to 99.67. Most non-U.S. currencies rose: the euro gained 0.20% against the dollar to 1.1590, sterling rose 0.05% to 1.3413, and the Australian dollar climbed 0.38% to 0.7070. The dollar rose 0.08% against the yen to 160.3540 and gained 0.02% against the Canadian dollar to 1.3991, while it fell 0.30% against the Swiss franc to 0.7947. The offshore yuan rose 45 basis points against the dollar to 6.7596.

China’s foreign exchange market operated generally steadily in May. Non-bank sectors, including enterprises and individuals, recorded net cross-border capital inflows of $62.5 billion, up slightly by 1% month on month. Banks recorded a foreign exchange settlement and sales surplus of $35.8 billion, down 11% month on month. Foreign investors were overall net buyers of domestic stocks and bonds.

South Korea’s Vice Finance Minister in charge of foreign exchange policy, Moon Jisung, recently met with U.S. Treasury officials to consult on foreign exchange market trends. Moon said the recent depreciation of the Korean won lacks reasonable support and has gone too far. The two sides agreed to work together to address won weakness.

Goldman Sachs: Why the #Fed Is Unlikely to Cut Rates This Year
#GoldmanSachs Research doesn’t expect the #FederalReserve to lower rates until next year. David Mericle, chief US economist, has pushed his forecast for the final two rate cuts for this cycle to June and December 2027 (from December 2026 and March 2027 previously).
US economic activity and labor market data “have been stronger than we anticipated in recent months, with job growth in particular picking up impressively,” Mericle writes.
Mericle’s team still anticipates that GDP growth will be somewhat below potential in the second half of this year as high oil prices weigh on spending. But our economists now expect the unemployment rate to rise only a touch further this year to 4.4%, down from a previous forecast of 4.6%.
The combined effects of tariffs, higher oil prices and other effects of the war in the Middle East, and artificial intelligence (AI) demand are expected to keep year-over-year core personal consumption expenditures inflation steady at above 3% throughout 2026. But fundamental drivers of inflation look softer. “As a result, we continue to expect inflation to fall to close to 2% in 2027, barring additional supply shocks,” Mericle writes.

Ag News Today: Tuesday, June 16, 2026

Energy Meltdown Deflates Grain Markets as Landmark U.S.-Iran Ceasefire Reopens Strait of Hormuz

The macroeconomic framework anchoring commodity prices has completely shifted. A surprise diplomatic breakthrough over the weekend led to a preliminary peace agreement between the United States and Iran, prompting a massive liquidation of the "war premium" across energy sectors. With the Strait of Hormuz poised to resume normal commercial shipping, grain futures are sliding lower, completely overshadowed by collapsing crude values and stable domestic crop conditions.

Macro Shockwave: Crude Plunges on Peace Agreement
Outside markets are processing their sharpest correction in months following the signing of a memorandum of understanding to end hostilities in the Middle East. The immediate unwinding of geopolitical risk completely deflated the energy complex, completely reversing the spring narrative.

Crude Benchmarks: West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude futures nose-dived to near $80.69 per barrel, while Brent crude slipped below the critical threshold to settle at $82.97. The rapid decline follows a near 5% single-day plunge on Monday, sending oil contracts to their lowest levels since early March.

The Hormuz Relief Valve: Traders are aggressively pricing in the gradual reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, which will return critical blocked crude supplies to global markets and normalize international shipping lanes.

Deflationary Squeeze on Fertilizer and Fuel
The sudden de-escalation of the Middle East conflict promises massive, immediate financial relief for upcoming fall inputs, though nearby operations are still dealing with high-cost old stock.

Input Pipeline Reset: Wholesale nitrogen markets are accelerating their downward trend. With a third of global seaborne fertilizer trade no longer facing transit blockades, June NOLA granular urea futures have extended their decline, keeping pressure on domestic retail distributor margins.

Refined Products Peak: On-farm retail diesel prices, which heavily penalized spring field operations at multi-year highs, are finally expected to trace crude lower. Analysts project the sudden $10 to $15 drop in underlying crude barrels will pull retail fuel averages down significantly over the coming weeks.

USDA Crop Progress: Quality Baseline Settles In
With national corn planting 100% finished, Monday afternoon's USDA Crop Progress report focused cleanly on emergence speeds and stable early-season health trends.

Corn: Seedling emergence has reached 94% nationwide. The USDA bumped the national corn condition rating up 1 point to 68% good-to-excellent. In individual state highlights, Iowa's crop is off to an exceptional start at 79% good-to-excellent, comfortably balancing out softer spots in the eastern Corn Belt. July corn futures closed soft, hovering around $4.14 per bushel.

Soybeans: National planting progress is essentially complete at 95%, with emergence jumping to 88% complete (well ahead of the 82% five-year average). Initial quality ratings ticked up slightly to 66% good-to-excellent. July bean futures are struggling to find solid ground near $11.15 as macro energy weakness prompts fund liquidation.

Winter Wheat: Harvest is accelerating rapidly due to hot, dry conditions in the Southern Plains, jumping 14 points over the week to reach 25% complete nationwide, far ahead of the 13% historical average. Texas is leading the charge at 75% cut, followed by Oklahoma at 73%. The national condition remains highly fractured at a low 27% good-to-excellent, with 45% of the crop locked in poor or very poor condition.

All Day: Monitoring cash basis adjustments at Western Corn Belt river terminals as local operators evaluate the drop in freight and energy costs.

3:00 PM CT: Traders watching macro fund flows to see if institutional money continues to rotate out of grains and into defensive hard metals.

The preliminary agreement announced over the weekend between the US and Iran has boosted risk appetite in global markets, but the cryptocurrency market has not seen a similarly strong sense of relief. In particular, expectations of a possible reopening of the Strait of Hormuz led to rapid price fluctuations in energy markets. While oil prices experienced a sharp decline, a more optimistic atmosphere emerged in Asian and US stock markets.

From a global markets perspective, this development means that one of the biggest uncertainties regarding energy supply has been reduced. The decline in oil prices has strengthened expectations that inflationary pressures may ease and central banks may have more room to act. Therefore, stocks, especially those in sectors sensitive to energy costs and Asian markets, reacted more quickly to the news of the agreement.

Cryptocurrencies Cautious
However, the picture in the cryptocurrency market remained more measured. Although Bitcoin attempted a recovery over the weekend, it failed to maintain its strength above the $66,000 region. Ethereum similarly recorded limited gains, while some altcoins with smaller market cap saw relatively stronger movements. This indicates that investors did not directly translate geopolitical optimism into a large buying wave in the crypto market.

Bitcoin 4-hour chart
There are several reasons why the market remains cautious. First, the agreement is not yet fully implementable, and the details are unclear, keeping investors in a wait-and-see mode. The fact that previous ceasefires and reconciliation announcements from the Middle East quickly fell apart has led the market to approach such developments with greater caution.

Secondly, strong internal catalysts to support an uptrend in the cryptocurrency market are currently limited. Without spot ETF inflows, institutional purchases, stablecoin liquidity, and a significant revival in on-chain activity, it seems unlikely that news of geopolitical easing alone will initiate a strong and lasting trend in Bitcoin.

Derivative markets are giving a similar message. Although there is an increase in open positions, the fact that funding rates remain low indicates that investors are not using aggressive leverage. In the options market, the change in the put/call balance reveals that some investors are still seeking protection against downside risks. However, the low implied volatility suggests that there is no panic in the market.

In conclusion, while expectations of a US-Iran agreement created significant relief in traditional markets, it had a more limited impact on the cryptocurrency market. A sustained rise in Bitcoin and altcoins may require more than just a decrease in geopolitical tensions. Investors will closely monitor whether the agreement is implemented, whether the decline in oil prices continues, expectations regarding the Fed, and liquidity inflows specific to the crypto market.

Oil is capitulating — and this may be the moment to pay attention

WTI has lost more than $14 in just a few days, falling from $91.62 last Thursday to $77.26 today. The immediate cause is the prospect of a peace agreement and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. But the author of this analysis points out an anomaly: before the war, oil was at $65. The conflict removed 10 to 12 million barrels per day from the market for approximately 100 days — more than a billion barrels that need to be rebuilt. The speed and magnitude of the current decline seem disconnected from this physical reality.

The signal is not fundamental — it is behavioral. The author identifies a classic capitulation pattern: short sellers celebrating their victory, bulls moving from denial to anger and then to abandonment. This type of dynamic, observable in the flow of comments and the speed of the drop, has historically been associated with market lows. Jeff Currie, former head of commodities at Goldman Sachs, adds a physical argument: flows through Hormuz will not normalize before the end of the year, and industrial buyers — such as German fuel importers — have deliberately emptied their stocks in anticipation of lower prices without yet restocking.

If the capitulation thesis is correct, the asymmetric risk on oil is starting to tilt upward. The market has already priced in a rapid reopening and normalization of supply — but neither is secured at this pace. The 60 million barrels that will come out of Hormuz upon reopening will create an initial flow, but the return of ships for new loadings will take time. For energy-related assets, a rebound in oil from these levels would quickly change the inflation calculation — partially invalidating the disinflationary narrative that bond markets have begun to integrate. The Fed tomorrow will view oil at $77 as an ally. It may not see it that low for very long.

The dynamic described here is one of the most reliable in behavioral market analysis: the moment when losers capitulate and winners parade is precisely when the move exhausts itself. Sellers at these levels are no longer speculators looking for a trade — they are longs being forced out. The selling pressure is emotional, not strategic. And when selling becomes emotional, it creates price levels that fundamentals do not justify — which then attracts opportunistic buyers. The dominant narrative “oil will continue to fall because peace is coming” may already be too consensual to remain profitable.

Source: Adam Button — Investing Live.

The 8 unresolved questions in Trump's Iran deal

Dave Lawler, Barak Ravid

The U.S.-Iran deal was signed electronically on Sunday by President Trump, Vice President JD Vance and Iranian parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, senior administration officials told reporters on Monday.

Why it matters: The deal is already testing whether Washington and Tehran can turn a battlefield pause into a broader settlement — starting with the Strait of Hormuz and then moving to the far thornier question of Iran's nuclear program.

State of play: A 60-day ceasefire extension is in effect, U.S. officials say, including in Lebanon. But the Strait of Hormuz is not expected to begin reopening until after a formal signing ceremony Friday in Geneva.

Vance, U.S. envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, Ghalibaf, and Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi are expected to meet Friday with Pakistani and Qatari mediators to discuss the next phase.

U.S. officials say the full text of the memorandum of understanding will be released in the next 24–48 hours, though Trump said it might not be published until after it's signed.

The big picture: Washington and Tehran are racing to shape the narrative around a deal that few people have actually read, but which markets, world leaders and ordinary citizens are already reacting to.

The U.S. says relief for Iran will be tied to performance. Iranian state media has described a more generous package.

Shipping companies say they're waiting for clearer guarantees from Tehran before sending cargo through the Strait of Hormuz.

Hawks in Washington and Israel are pressing the White House to explain exactly what Iran is getting — and what happens if nuclear talks fail.

Breaking it down: Here are eight key questions that are still swirling around the deal.

1. Is the deal actually in effect?

The MOU was signed electronically on Sunday, with a more formal signing ceremony to be held on Friday in Switzerland.

The 60-day extension of the ceasefire took immediate effect, but the strait is not yet fully open.

While Trump announced the "immediate" lifting of the U.S. blockade and opening of the strait on Sunday, he later said it would open on Friday once the ceremony takes place.

A U.S. defense official said the military was ordered to prepare to lift the blockade Friday.

Trump claimed Monday that ships were already moving, though Iranian state media said the status of the strait was unchanged.

2. Will the strait be truly open?

The U.S. side has consistently said the deal would open the strait without tolls or any other restrictions.

A regional diplomat involved in the mediation told Axios last week that the deal called for shipping volumes through the strait to return to prewar levels within 30 days.

But Iranian officials have told state media the strait won't simply return to "pre-war status," and that Iran will retain some level of control.

The Fars news agency reported that Iran had agreed not to impose tolls during the 60 days, but would begin charging safety and environmental fees after that.

The intrigue: A senior administration official said there would be a "regional dialogue" on the future of the strait and how to ensure it will never be closed again.

State of play: Shipping companies are moving cautiously.

Maersk and others have said they're waiting for more clarity and guarantees of safety, and some analysts doubt volumes will actually return to the pre-war norm anytime soon.

A senior administration official told reporters the U.S. expects volumes to increase significantly over the next two weeks, though probably not to return to normal.

"Some crews want to see a little bit more stability for the next couple of days, maybe the next couple of weeks," the official said.

3. What does Iran get?

Both sides agree that Iran gets two big things: an end to the fighting and sanctions waivers to allow oil exports.

That alone would generate much-needed revenue for Tehran, but Iranian state media has claimed the government is also getting billions of dollars in frozen funds just for signing.

A senior U.S. official adamantly denied that, saying Iran would only get access to those funds based on a "pay for performance model." The full economic benefits of the deal, the U.S. side says, depend on signing a more detailed nuclear accord.

Some skeptics of the deal have raised concerns there might be side agreements that give Iran access to cash immediately, though the White House calls that "misinformation."

Yes, but: The senior administration official said the U.S. was prepared to make some "small gestures" early in the process on frozen funds and sanctions relief if Iran made similar "gestures" reflecting their willingness to comply with the deal.

4. Do they agree on what's been agreed?

The U.S. and Iran are offering conflicting accounts of what the deal requires — and what each side gets in return.

That's in part because the negotiations were handled largely indirectly, through mediators, and the MOU is a broad political understanding rather than a detailed treaty.

That could leave enough ambiguity for both sides to claim they got what they needed, even if they have different expectations about what happens next.

"I am somewhat concerned that Iran's view of the agreement seems different than what the American negotiating team is claiming," said Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), a hawkish Trump ally.

One of the senior administration officials claimed the discrepancies were actually due to Iran overselling the deal for domestic consumption.

5. Will we see the text?

Much of this confusion could be cleared up if the parties or the mediators simply published the full text of the MOU.

In the Monday briefing, a senior administration official said that would happen in the next 24–48 hours. Trump said it might not happen until after Friday.

6. Will Israel comply?

The agreement is receiving criticism from across the political spectrum in Israel, less than four months before an election. That's in part because it requires Israel to observe a ceasefire in Lebanon.

Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz has said Israel will not withdraw from the parts of southern Lebanon it occupies or give up its ability to respond to Hezbollah attacks. If Iran strikes Israel over the war in Lebanon, Katz said, Israel will hit Iran "with full force."

A senior U.S. official said the deal does not require an Israeli withdrawal and would not create a "one-way ceasefire," meaning Israel could respond if Hezbollah attacks.

But Israeli officials worry their freedom to operate in Lebanon will be sharply curtailed.

After an Israeli strike in Beirut on Sunday nearly derailed the deal, Trump told Axios that Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu had "no fucking judgment."

Trump said Monday that he hopes to "solve" the situation in Lebanon and that "we'll have to have a little chat with Hezbollah about this."

7. Will there ever be a nuclear deal?

The MOU is designed to launch 60 days of nuclear negotiations.

All of the nuclear elements of the deal are dependent on a much more technical final agreement, as is the bulk of the sanctions relief Iran hopes to receive.

U.S. officials acknowledge it will be very difficult to achieve such a deal, given the mutual distrust and how difficult it was to get the much less detailed MOU.

Witkoff and Kushner traveled to Oak Ridge, Tennessee, earlier this month to meet with nuclear experts who would play a role in such negotiations.

What's next: Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, one of the mediators, said there would be meetings "this week" to set the stage for nuclear negotiations.

8. Will the war resume without a nuclear deal?

U.S. officials say they will not withdraw the forces sent to the region unless there is a nuclear deal.

One official argued that the damage imposed on Iran increased the chances of a deal, and said Trump still has "tools in his arsenal" if diplomacy fails.

Iranian officials, meanwhile, claim Trump was desperate to end the war and that Tehran now has the leverage.

What to watch: "I think we'll know over the next two to three weeks whether those understandings will turn into an actual agreement," a senior administration official told reporters.

What they're saying: In response to these questions and to criticism of the deal from hawks, a senior administration official told Axios that Trump said from the outset that his goals were to prevent Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon or keeping its highly enriched uranium.

"This deal accomplishes both objectives, and the United States, our allies, and our troops in the region will be safer as a result."
